does: This paper introduces MLLMEraser, a training-free framework for removing specific knowledge from multimodal large language models at test time through activation steering. Rather than retraining or updating parameters, the method constructs multimodal erasure directions by contrasting adversarially perturbed image-text pairs that recall target knowledge against modified pairs where that knowledge is erased. The framework applies these directions selectively via an input-aware steering mechanism that determines when erasure should activate, enabling dynamic removal of memorized private data, outdated information, or harmful content without permanently modifying model weights or degrading performance on retained knowledge.
connects: This work extends the emerging field of machine unlearning, which has primarily focused on training-based approaches like gradient ascent and preference optimization for language models. MLLMEraser adapts activation steering techniques—previously developed for controlled generation and value editing in unimodal models—to the multimodal setting where both visual and textual information must be simultaneously processed. The paper builds on recent insights about test-time model control and the interpretability of internal activations, while addressing limitations of expensive retraining-based unlearning methods that either irreversibly modify models or create knowledge distortions, extending these concerns to the multimodal domain where memorization spans vision and language modalities.
enables: This approach opens pathways for practical deployment-stage knowledge management in MLLMs without retraining costs or irreversibility constraints. Future work could explore how activation steering generalizes to other modalities beyond vision-language, develop stronger adversarial perturbation strategies to create more robust erasure directions, or investigate selective unlearning of fine-grained concepts rather than entire knowledge categories. The test-time nature of the method enables regulatory compliance mechanisms where harmful content or private data can be dynamically removed based on context or user preferences, while the input-aware mechanism could be extended to more sophisticated routing strategies that preserve beneficial information while erasing only specified targets, potentially enabling personalized or jurisdiction-specific model behavior.
